name: Major Holley
abstract: Major "Mule" Holley Jr. (July 10, 1924 – October 25, 1990) was an American jazz double bassist.

concerts.label: Newport Jazz Festival: New York Jazz Repertory Company
Kool Jazz Festival: Benny Carter and Illinois Jacquet

about_quotes.speaker: Cliff Leeman
about_quotes.evidence: I was doing the thing with Major Holley... Major Holley on base; Ed Hubbie on trombone... we had a little trio with Dick Wellstood

about_quotes.speaker: Kenny Burrell
about_quotes.evidence: I had been working with Major Holley and Bill English, so we had a certain groove going in terms of the time and sub-pathical.
about_quotes.doc_title: Kenny Burrell — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Jackie Williams
about_quotes.evidence: They’re playing for Tiny Bradshaw... We worked together quite a bit even since and after that you know.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jackie Williams interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, October 18, 1995

about_quotes.speaker: Dianne Reeves
about_quotes.evidence: And sometimes it would be George Duvivier on bass or Major Holley... I would come there with all my music together... Clark just stayed in touch. And from that point on we just continued to work together
about_quotes.doc_title: Dianne Reeves interviewed by Monk Rowe and Michael Woods, Clinton, New York, October 13, 2001

about_quotes.speaker: Pierre Boussaguet
about_quotes.evidence: That was a record under the name of Sweets, a quintet with Eddie “Lockjaw” Davis, Oliver Jackson drums, beautiful piano player Gerry Wiggins, and Major Holley on bass.
about_quotes.doc_title: Pierre Boussaguet interviewed by Matthieu Jaffre, Aix en Provence, France, July 28, 2020

about_quotes.speaker: Glenn Zottola
about_quotes.evidence: We jam for 45 minutes. He comes up to me and he says, “I love your playing, can you leave tomorrow for the road?” ... So I leave, so I get to the gig
about_quotes.doc_title: Glenn Zottola interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, December 7, 2020
